curator: Fable 5 (Build-15, S8) — every load-bearing figure verified against its source at curation time (KG-integrity acceptance step, team round 3); verification method+date recorded per seed
schema_note: Adopted round-3 Q1 schema: two-axis result_type x scope (GPT), proof_strength/tested_bound/negative_result (unanimous), provenance + related_features (Grok), canonical_statement in M3 grammar where expressible (Gemini). matches_templates drives the deterministic novelty pass: 'TEMPLATE' covers the family, 'TEMPLATE:slot=val' pins a slot.

seeds.title: Terras 1976 — almost all n have finite stopping time
seeds.statement: The set of positive integers with finite stopping time has natural density 1; the density F(k) of n with stopping time at most k is computable from the distribution of parity vectors and tends to 1 as k grows (central-limit argument over parity prefixes).
seeds.canonical_statement: at least P% of n <= N have stopping time sigma(n) <= T (P computable from parity-vector counting for every T)
seeds.source_citation: R. Terras, A stopping time problem on the positive integers, Acta Arithmetica 30 (1976), 241-252
seeds.author: Terras

verification.note: Moeller 1978 criticized details of the original proof; gaps later filled — recorded for honesty

seeds.title: Terras 1976 — parity prefixes are equidistributed mod 2^k
seeds.statement: The first k parity bits of the trajectory of n under the Terras map are determined by n mod 2^k, and each of the 2^k possible parity prefixes is realized by exactly one residue class mod 2^k (equidistribution of parity vectors).
seeds.source_citation: R. Terras, A stopping time problem on the positive integers, Acta Arithmetica 30 (1976), 241-252
seeds.author: Terras

verification.method: web (same source as terras-1976-stopping-density) + the M1 census itself reproduces the 2^-k prefix densities (Build-13)

seeds.title: Everett 1977 — independent almost-all finite stopping time
seeds.statement: Independent, contemporaneous proof that almost all positive integers have finite stopping time (trajectory drops below its start) under the 3x+1 iteration.
seeds.source_citation: C. J. Everett, Iteration of the number-theoretic function f(2n)=n, f(2n+1)=3n+2, Advances in Mathematics 25 (1977), 42-45
seeds.author: Everett

verification.method: cross-checked against the Lagarias surveys (canonical bibliography)

seeds.title: Lagarias 1985 — the 3x+1 problem survey
seeds.statement: Canonical survey of the 3x+1 problem: history, equivalent formulations (Terras map, Syracuse function), stochastic heuristics for trajectory decay, and the state of partial results as of 1985.
seeds.source_citation: J. C. Lagarias, The 3x+1 problem and its generalizations, American Mathematical Monthly 92 (1985), 3-23
seeds.author: Lagarias

verification.method: standard reference, cross-checked via Lagarias 2003 annotated bibliography

seeds.title: Tao 2019 — almost all orbits attain almost bounded values
seeds.statement: For any function f(N) tending to infinity, Col_min(N) < f(N) for almost all N in the sense of LOGARITHMIC density (so e.g. almost all orbits dip below log log N). Scope caveat is load-bearing: logarithmic density, not natural density, and 'almost all', not 'all'.
seeds.source_citation: T. Tao, Almost all orbits of the Collatz map attain almost bounded values, Forum of Mathematics, Pi 10 (2022), e12; arXiv:1909.03562 (2019)
seeds.author: Tao

seeds.title: Korec 1994 — almost all orbits dip below n^theta
seeds.statement: For any theta > ln3/ln4 (approx. 0.7924), Col_min(n) <= n^theta for almost all n in the sense of natural density. The record 'how low do almost all orbits go' result from 1994 until Tao 2019.
seeds.source_citation: I. Korec, A density estimate for the 3x+1 problem, Mathematica Slovaca 44 (1994), 85-89
seeds.author: Korec

verification.method: web (cited with statement in Tao's 2019 paper/blog)

seeds.title: Krasikov–Lagarias 2003 — at least x^0.84 integers reach 1
seeds.statement: The number of integers n <= x whose orbit reaches 1 is at least x^0.84 for sufficiently large x (difference-inequality / tree-search method). Still the best lower bound of this form.
seeds.source_citation: I. Krasikov, J. C. Lagarias, Bounds for the 3x+1 problem using difference inequalities, Acta Arithmetica 109 (2003), 237-258
seeds.author: Krasikov-Lagarias

seeds.title: Barina — convergence verified for all n <= 2^71
seeds.statement: Every starting value n <= 2^71 (approx. 2.36e21) converges to the trivial cycle, by distributed GPU verification (published baseline 2^68 in J. Supercomputing 2021; the ongoing project's verified frontier is 2^71). Path records were checked en route; four new path records found. This is the computational frontier: any claim of a counterexample below 2^71 contradicts this result.
seeds.source_citation: D. Barina, Convergence verification of the Collatz problem, The Journal of Supercomputing 77 (2021), 2681-2688; improved-limit follow-up + project page pcbarina.fit.vutbr.cz
seeds.author: Barina

verification.method: web (Springer 2021 paper + project page + improved-verification-limit paper)

seeds.title: Eliahou 1993 — nontrivial cycles have length >= 17,087,915
seeds.statement: Any nontrivial cycle of the 3x+1 map has length at least 17,087,915 (via the continued-fraction expansion of log2(3) combined with the then-current verification bound). Improves Garner 1981 (35,400).
seeds.source_citation: S. Eliahou, The 3x+1 problem: new lower bounds on nontrivial cycle lengths, Discrete Mathematics 118 (1993), 45-56
seeds.author: Eliahou

seeds.title: Hercher 2022 — no nontrivial m-cycles for m <= 91
seeds.statement: There are no nontrivial Collatz m-cycles with m <= 91 (a nontrivial cycle must have at least 92 ascent-descent blocks). Extends the Steiner (m=1) and Simons-de Weger line; combined with verification bounds this severely constrains any hypothetical cycle.
seeds.source_citation: C. Hercher, There are no Collatz-m-cycles with m <= 91, arXiv:2201.00406 (2022); J. Integer Sequences
seeds.author: Hercher

seeds.title: Steiner 1977 — no nontrivial 1-cycles
seeds.statement: There is no nontrivial 1-cycle (circuit): a cycle consisting of a single run of odd steps followed by a single run of even steps must be the trivial {1,2,4} cycle. Proof uses Baker-type bounds on linear forms in logarithms — the start of the m-cycle exclusion line.
seeds.source_citation: R. P. Steiner, A theorem on the Syracuse problem, Proc. 7th Manitoba Conference on Numerical Mathematics and Computing (1977), 553-559
seeds.author: Steiner

verification.method: cross-checked against Hercher 2022 introduction + Lagarias surveys (lineage m=1 -> 68 -> 91)

seeds.title: Elementary — nu2(3n+1) follows the geometric law 2^-k
seeds.statement: For odd n, the 2-adic valuation nu2(3n+1) = k is decided by n mod 2^(k+1): exactly one odd residue class mod 2^(k+1) yields each k >= 1, so the density of odd n with nu2(3n+1) = k is exactly 2^-k. A provable residue identity, NOT an empirical property of Collatz dynamics — any 'conjecture' restating it is known mathematics.
seeds.canonical_statement: the fraction of odd n <= N with nu2(3n+1) = K is within EPS pp of 2^-K (provable for every K by residue counting)
seeds.source_citation: Elementary 2-adic residue counting (folklore; implicit in Terras 1976's parity analysis and standard in the Lagarias surveys)
seeds.author: folklore

verification.method: proved directly: n odd, 3n+1 even; nu2(3n+1)=k iff n = (2^k * u - 1)/3 pattern, one odd class mod 2^(k+1); PS-checked against the M1 census (2^-k densities, Build-13/14)

seeds.title: Elementary/Terras — small stopping times are residue-pinned
seeds.statement: sigma(n) = 1 exactly for even n (density 1/2) and sigma(n) = 3 exactly for n = 1 (mod 4) (density 1/4); generally membership of {n : sigma(n) <= k} is decided by n mod 2^k (parity prefixes), so cumulative stopping-time frequencies at small thresholds are exactly computable — they are known mathematics, not discoveries.
seeds.canonical_statement: at least P% of n <= N have stopping time sigma(n) <= T (P residue-computable at small T)
seeds.source_citation: Elementary; the general parity-prefix mechanism is Terras 1976
seeds.author: Terras/folklore

verification.method: proved directly (even n halve below n in one step; n=1 mod 4: 3n+1 = 4 mod 12 halves twice to (3n+1)/4 < n); PS-verified to 10,000 in tests/m3-conjecture-verify.ps1 (Build-14)

seeds.title: The Collatz conjecture (open)
seeds.statement: Every positive integer eventually reaches 1 under iteration of the Collatz map (n -> 3n+1 for odd n, n -> n/2 for even n). OPEN since circa 1937 (Lothar Collatz). The target statement of this research thread — every other seed in this pack is partial progress or a constraint on its failure modes.
seeds.source_citation: L. Collatz (unpublished, 1937); J. C. Lagarias (ed.), The Ultimate Challenge: The 3x+1 Problem, AMS 2010
seeds.author: Collatz

seeds.title: Failure trichotomy + empty status below 2^71
seeds.statement: Every orbit does exactly one of: reach the trivial {1,2,4} cycle, enter a nontrivial cycle, or diverge to infinity. No nontrivial cycle and no divergent orbit is known; neither exists below the 2^71 verification frontier, and any nontrivial cycle is constrained by the Eliahou/Hercher results.
seeds.source_citation: Standard; see the Lagarias surveys and Barina's verification papers
seeds.author: Lagarias (survey)

verification.method: logical trichotomy (definitional) + computational status from web-verified Barina seeds

seeds.title: OEIS A006577 — total stopping times
seeds.statement: OEIS A006577: number of steps for n to reach 1 under the Collatz map (the total stopping time sigma_inf). The canonical reference sequence for total-stopping-time values, records, and cross-references.
seeds.author: OEIS

verification.method: OEIS lookup (sequence id + definition); M1 census values cross-check (sigma_inf(27)=111)

seeds.title: OEIS A006884 — max-excursion record-setters
seeds.statement: OEIS A006884: starting values n whose Collatz trajectory reaches a higher maximum than any smaller start (max-excursion record-setters), e.g. 27 with peak 9232. The reference sequence for peak records.
seeds.author: OEIS

verification.method: OEIS lookup; M1 census cross-check (peak(27)=9232, peak(9663)=27,114,424 record table, Build-13)

seeds.title: Lagarias 2003 — annotated 3x+1 bibliography
seeds.statement: Annotated bibliography of the 3x+1 problem: the comprehensive map of the literature (later updated through 2010 in The Ultimate Challenge). The canonical place to check whether a 3x+1 result exists before claiming novelty.
seeds.source_citation: J. C. Lagarias, The 3x+1 problem: an annotated bibliography (1963-1999), arXiv:math/0309224; part II arXiv:math/0608208
seeds.author: Lagarias

seeds.title: Garner 1981 — early nontrivial-cycle length bound
seeds.statement: Any nontrivial cycle of the 3x+1 map has length at least 35,400 — the early continued-fraction cycle bound, superseded by Eliahou 1993 (17,087,915). Kept as lineage: bounds of this family scale with the computational verification frontier.
seeds.source_citation: L. E. Garner, On the Collatz 3n+1 algorithm, Proceedings of the AMS 82 (1981), 19-22
seeds.author: Garner
